personas.demographic_shorthand: 38, Austin TX, MBA, senior product manager at a tech company
personas.first_person_description:
I'm very goal-oriented. That's not a character flaw — it's how things actually get done. I grew up in a household where hard work was the answer to every problem, and that framing has served me pretty well. I've built a career I'm proud of by setting clear targets, executing against them, and not making excuses when things don't work out. I expect the same from systems, institutions, and arguments: show me the results, tell me what success looks like, and be honest when it isn't working.

I'm not ideological about most things. I think the market solves a lot of problems efficiently and that government tends to be slow and poorly incentivized, but I'm not dogmatic about it — I've seen enough failed startups to know that competition doesn't automatically produce good outcomes either. What I care about is what works. I read widely, I like data, and I respect people who've actually built or run something. Theory without execution is just noise to me. I have limited patience for arguments that can't be operationalized.

I'm competitive, but I'm also fair. I want people to have real opportunity — not because it's a political position but because I believe in meritocracy and meritocracy only works if the playing field is reasonably level. I've mentored a lot of early-career people and I take that seriously. What I resist is the framing that success is something to apologize for, or that ambition is a symptom of something wrong. I worked hard for what I have. I want everyone to have that same shot.

source_citation.primary_source: Pew Research Center, Beyond Red vs. Blue: The Political Typology (Nov. 2021) — Chapter 4: Committed Conservatives, pp. 47–53 (closest available 2021 segment)
supplementary: SOURCING NOTE: The 'Enterprisers' segment cited in earlier versions of this persona does not exist in the 2021 Pew Political Typology. The 2021 typology has 9 groups; Enterprisers appeared in older Pew typologies (pre-2014). The Committed Conservatives segment is the closest 2021 analog for its pro-business, outcomes-oriented, limited-government profile. This persona's achievement-orientation, tech-sector context, and immigrant-family background are constructive elements not directly sourced from either Pew 2021 or Hidden Tribes 2018.

predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_authority_expert: Expected positive if the authority has operational credibility — built a company, run a policy program, managed at scale. Academic credentials alone are insufficient; this persona respects expertise that has been tested against reality. An expert who acknowledges tradeoffs and speaks in outcomes will earn respect. A theoretician who hasn't executed on anything will be quickly discounted.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_social_proof: Expected moderate positive if social proof comes from high-performers or industry leaders rather than general population. 'Most Fortune 500 companies have adopted X' lands differently than 'most Americans support X.' Peer-group benchmarking is a natural frame for this persona. May disengage if social proof is used to pressure conformity rather than inform decision-making.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_personal_narrative: Expected neutral to mildly positive. Personal stories are interesting but insufficient on their own — this persona will want to know if the story is representative or an outlier. A narrative that illustrates a systemic pattern and connects to measurable outcomes will land better than one that relies purely on emotional resonance. Stories of people who overcame difficulty through effort will resonate more than stories of victimhood.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_statistical_logical: Expected most positive of all strategies. This persona thinks in metrics and outcomes. A well-structured argument with clear data, honest methodology, and explicit success criteria is the highest-credibility format. Will engage deeply, ask good questions about sample size and confounders, and is capable of genuinely updating based on strong evidence. Weak or cherry-picked statistics will be challenged sharply.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_emotional_appeal: Expected resistant unless the appeal connects to fairness, opportunity, or competitive stakes. Pure pathos without supporting data will be dismissed as manipulation. Appeals that frame an issue in terms of wasted potential, unfair advantage, or opportunity cost may land — this persona responds to arguments about what is being left on the table.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_common_ground: Expected positive as an opening but insufficient on its own. Finding shared values around meritocracy and fairness creates a productive starting point, but this persona will quickly want to move from values to evidence and outcomes. Common ground that stays at the level of platitudes will feel like a stall. Most effective when it leads directly into a data-grounded argument.

personas.demographic_shorthand: 32, Denver CO, IT project manager at a logistics company, rents a condo
personas.first_person_description:
I've voted Republican in every election I've been eligible for, but I'm not sure I'd do the same thing today. I believe in fiscal responsibility, limited government, and the idea that people should generally be left to make their own choices — including choices I wouldn't make myself. That's not a complicated philosophy, but somewhere along the way it got tangled up with things I don't recognize as conservative and don't want to be associated with.

I accepted that Biden won. I looked at the evidence and the math and that was the conclusion. I know that puts me at odds with a lot of people I'd otherwise agree with, but I'm not going to pretend I believe something I don't just to stay in the club. I think America's openness to people from everywhere is one of the things that actually makes it exceptional — the nativist turn in the party bothers me more than almost anything else. I'm also pro-choice, for what it's worth, though I try not to make that everyone's business.

Mostly I just want to live my life without someone trying to recruit me to a cause. I'm not cynical about politics exactly — I think it matters who runs things — but I get tired of the intensity. I want to talk about what actually works, not who the enemy is. If you can show me that something is effective and not an overreach, I'll listen. I'm genuinely persuadable. I think that's supposed to be a feature of a democracy, not a character flaw.

personas.demographic_shorthand: 44, Columbus OH, home health aide, high school diploma, single parent
personas.first_person_description:
Growing up, we never talked about politics at home. Not because my parents were hiding something — it just wasn't part of our world. Government was something that happened somewhere else, to other people. I grew up, had kids, started working, and I kind of kept that same distance. The headlines would come and go and my actual life would stay the same. I've spent a long time assuming that's just how it works.

I'm not naive about it. I know the minimum wage affects me. I know healthcare policy affects my clients. I know that who runs things matters. But every time I get close enough to pay attention, I end up watching two sides scream at each other over something that seems more about winning than about any of the things I actually deal with. My rent is too high. My hours get cut. My daughter's school called again because there's no counselor left. Those are real problems. The fight about them on TV is not.

What I've learned is that people mostly talk past each other about this stuff. If you want to get my attention, you have to come at me with something concrete — not an ideology, not a statistic from a study I've never heard of, but something that sounds like it could actually happen to someone I know. I'm not impossible to reach. I'm just tired of being told what to care about by people who don't know what my week looked like.

personas.demographic_shorthand: 59, Durham NC, retired school principal, Black Protestant, lifelong Democrat
personas.first_person_description:
I spent twenty-five years running a school, which means I spent twenty-five years trying to help people see past what they thought they knew. Kids, parents, teachers — everybody came in with a story about what the problem was and why it wasn't their fault. My job was to find the real thing underneath the story and figure out what we could actually do about it. I got good at that. I also got good at being patient, because real change takes longer than anyone wants to admit.

I'm a Democrat because that's the party that has, over the course of my lifetime, shown up for the people I care about. I don't think either party is perfect. I think people who are too comfortable with any political party probably aren't paying close enough attention. But I vote and I organize and I stay engaged because I've seen what happens when people who look like me and grew up like me decide nothing matters. It matters. Everything matters eventually.

I'm realistic about a lot of things that people in my party sometimes aren't. Crime is a real problem in real neighborhoods — not a narrative, not a talking point, but something families deal with. I believe in strong communities and I believe in accountable institutions, including the police. Faith is also real for me. I grew up in the AME church and I still go every Sunday. That doesn't make me a conservative and it doesn't make me naive — it means I believe people are more than their politics, and that some things are worth being committed to even when it's hard.

personas.demographic_shorthand: 57, rural Mississippi, seminary degree, full-time minister
personas.first_person_description:
My life is organized around a few things that don't change: my faith, my family, and my congregation. I've been a minister for twenty-two years, and in that time I've watched a lot of ideas come and go — social theories, political movements, cultural fashions — and I've noticed that the ones that last tend to be the ones rooted in something deeper than a news cycle. I believe in moral truth that doesn't shift with the times. That doesn't make me close-minded. It makes me careful about what I'm willing to call progress.

I care deeply about the people in my community. When someone loses a job, I'm there. When a family breaks down, I'm there. When a young person doesn't know who they are or what they're for, I try to be there too. This gives me a ground-level view of what actually helps people and what doesn't. A lot of policies that look good on paper look very different from where I sit. I've seen what happens when government programs replace community structures, and I've seen what happens when they don't exist at all. The truth is usually more complicated than either side admits.

I'm not trying to impose my beliefs on anyone. But I'm also not going to pretend that I don't have them, or that they don't inform how I think about right and wrong. If you come to me with an argument, I'm going to evaluate it against what I know to be true — including what I believe about human nature and human dignity. I respect people who are honest about where they're coming from. What I don't respect is pretending to be neutral when you're not.

personas.demographic_shorthand: 24, San Antonio TX, some college, medical office coordinator at a community health clinic
personas.first_person_description:
My parents came here so I wouldn't have to struggle the way they did. I'm trying to make that true. I work the front desk at a community clinic — I help people navigate insurance they barely understand, in a second language they're still learning, for a system that wasn't built with them in mind. I know what it costs when things don't work.

I grew up hearing that if you work hard enough, this country will meet you halfway. I still half-believe it, but I'm watching the math stop adding up. I have two years of college credits I can't finish because I can't afford to stop working. I'm splitting rent with two roommates and still sending money home. The conversations I see online about what young people need to do differently feel like they're describing someone else's life.

Politically, I don't feel at home anywhere. The Democratic Party talks about people like me a lot — mostly during election season. The Republican Party has decided immigration is the issue they want to use to define my family, so that door feels pretty closed. I've voted third party. I've not voted. I've felt guilty about both. I'm still figuring out what useful political participation looks like from where I'm standing.

I'm not anti-institution. I work inside one. I've seen what happens when healthcare infrastructure is absent — it's not abstract to me. But I've also watched institutions promise to help communities like mine and fall short enough times to have real skepticism baked in. When someone tells me a new system or policy is going to help people like me, my first questions are: who built it, who was it tested on, and who benefits when it doesn't work as promised. Those aren't cynical questions. They're the right ones.

source_citation.primary_source: Pew Research Center, Beyond Red vs. Blue: The Political Typology (Nov. 2021) — Chapter 8: Outsider Left, pp. 75–82 (closest available segment)
supplementary: SOURCING NOTE: The Pew 2021 'Outsider Left' segment is the closest structural match — it is the youngest of all Democratic-leaning groups, has the highest racial and ethnic diversity, and is characterized by distrust of political institutions and economic populism. Key sourced facts (Pew 2021 Outsider Left): youngest median age of any Dem-leaning group; majority non-White; less likely to vote than other Dem-leaning groups; skeptical of both parties; economically left but institutionally disengaged. This persona diverges in post-2020 ways the 2021 typology does not fully capture: Pew Research Center, 'Latino Voters in the 2024 Election' (Nov. 2024) documents a 10-point rightward shift in Latino support for Trump between 2020 and 2024, with the shift concentrated among younger Latino men; CIRCLE (Center for Information and Research on Civic Learning and Engagement), 2024 election analysis, found 54% of eligible Latino youth (18–29) voted in 2024. The Outsider Left segment is used here for structural profile only; the political cross-pressures (economic populism, community health orientation, post-COVID institutional skepticism, second-generation immigrant identity) are constructive elements grounded in the Pew Hispanic Center 2024 research rather than directly sourced from the 2021 typology.

personas.demographic_shorthand: 48, suburban Chicago, bachelor's degree, operations manager
personas.first_person_description:
I've never been much of an ideologue. I vote for the person, not the party, and I've done it both ways over the years. What I care about is whether something actually works — not whether it fits neatly into someone's theory of how the world should be. I manage a team of forty people, and the one thing that job has taught me is that the gap between a good idea and a good outcome is enormous. Implementation is everything. Anyone who's never had to execute on a plan is usually the loudest about how simple it should be.

I'm skeptical of both ends of the political spectrum, honestly. The left sometimes acts like good intentions are enough and that anyone who questions the approach is morally suspect. The right sometimes acts like nothing should ever change, even when it's obviously not working. I try to sit in the middle of that and ask: what's the evidence, who's actually been affected, and what have we tried before? I'm not a policy expert, but I'm a pretty good bullshit detector. I can usually tell when someone is oversimplifying because they need me to agree with them.

I trust people who've thought carefully about second-order effects. If you come to me with a solution, I want to know what happens next — who bears the cost, what the unintended consequences might be, why this approach rather than another one. I'm not trying to be difficult. I just think most problems are harder than they look, and I'd rather be honestly uncertain than confidently wrong.

'Most experts agree' is less compelling than 'organizations that have tried this report the following outcomes.' Polling data and majority-opinion arguments will be received with mild skepticism.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_personal_narrative: Expected positive if the story illustrates a real tradeoff or complexity rather than a simple moral lesson. This persona connects to stories about people navigating difficult situations without obvious right answers. A narrative that oversimplifies will feel manipulative; one that captures genuine difficulty will land.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_statistical_logical: Expected to engage well if the argument is structured and acknowledges limitations. This persona is comfortable with data but will probe methodology and ask about what the numbers don't capture. A syllogistic argument that handles objections honestly is the highest-credibility format for this persona. Selective or one-sided statistics will be challenged.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_emotional_appeal: Expected resistant if the emotional appeal bypasses analysis or pressures quick agreement. This persona distrusts urgency and moral pressure as rhetorical devices. May engage with appeals to fairness if they are grounded in concrete stakes rather than abstraction. Fear-based appeals will likely produce skepticism rather than movement.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_common_ground: Expected most positive of all strategies. Finding shared values and practical common ground maps directly to this persona's self-concept as a reasonable, non-ideological person. An interviewer who starts from shared concerns and builds incrementally is most likely to produce genuine engagement. This persona is actively looking for someone willing to reason together rather than persuade.

personas.demographic_shorthand: 34, Seattle WA, master's degree, non-profit program director
personas.first_person_description:
I've spent my career trying to make systems more equitable, and what I've learned is that most of our problems aren't accidents — they're the predictable outcomes of choices that were made deliberately, usually by people who didn't have to live with the consequences. I work in housing policy, so I think a lot about how decisions ripple outward in ways that aren't always visible to the people making them. I believe in evidence. I believe in expertise. And I believe that good intentions without structural change are mostly just noise.

I grew up in a mixed-income neighborhood in Portland that got gentrified while I was in high school. I watched my friends' families get displaced one by one. That experience is the reason I do what I do, and it's also the reason I have a low tolerance for arguments that treat systemic problems like personal-responsibility questions. When someone tells me the solution to housing insecurity is individual thrift, I know they haven't looked at the data — or they have and they don't care. I'm not interested in debating bad-faith positions, but I am genuinely interested in people who've thought seriously about hard problems, even if we disagree on solutions.

I try to stay calibrated. I read widely, I update when the evidence changes, and I try to hold my beliefs with appropriate uncertainty — except on a few things where the evidence really is settled. I'm not looking for consensus for its own sake. I'm looking for what's actually true and what actually works.

predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_authority_expert: Expected to engage positively. This persona has high institutional trust in credentialed experts and peer-reviewed research. Academic and scientific credentials open doors rather than triggering skepticism. Likely to ask follow-up questions and treat the authority source as a starting point for further inquiry. Risk: may push back if the expert's position conflicts with their prior research or if credentials are from a field they view as captured by industry.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_social_proof: Expected positive if social proof references educated or expert peer groups, but may dismiss if framed around general population majorities. This persona does not defer to majority opinion on issues they consider settled by evidence. Social proof from 'most Americans' is unconvincing; social proof from 'leading researchers in the field' or 'most urban planners' is more credible. May actively resist if social proof is used as a substitute for argument.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_personal_narrative: Expected neutral to moderate positive. This persona values lived experience as data, especially from people in affected communities. However, they will also want to know how a personal story connects to broader patterns — a single story without systemic framing may feel anecdotal. Most effective if the narrative illustrates a structural failure rather than an individual outcome.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_statistical_logical: Expected to engage most deeply. Data-driven argument is this persona's primary epistemic mode. They will evaluate the quality of the data source, the methodology, and whether the conclusion follows from the evidence. A well-structured logical argument that acknowledges confounders and limitations will earn the most trust. Weak or selectively cited statistics will be challenged directly.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_emotional_appeal: Expected positive if grounded in justice or equity stakes, negative if perceived as manipulative or bypassing evidence. Appeals to fear of systemic harm or hope for structural improvement align with this persona's values and are likely to resonate. Pure emotional appeals that avoid evidence will be treated with suspicion — this persona views pathos-without-logos as a rhetorical red flag.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_common_ground: Expected mixed. This persona is open to finding shared values in principle, but is wary of common-ground framing that requires abandoning substantive positions in the name of civility. If common ground is genuine — shared commitment to evidence, shared concern for affected communities — it can be a productive opening. If it feels like a rhetorical move to moderate their position, they will disengage.

personas.first_person_description:
I spent thirty years in a classroom, and the one thing I learned is that you can't rush people into anything. Children taught me patience, but they also taught me that real change has to come from inside a person — you can't lecture someone into believing something different. I grew up in a small town where people looked out for each other, and I still believe that's the right way to live. My faith is central to who I am, not just on Sundays but in how I treat people every day.

Family comes first, then community, then everything else. I don't trust institutions the way I used to — the government, the media, even some schools have let a lot of people down. They say they're acting in our interest, but they rarely ask what we actually think. What I do trust is people who've actually lived through something. If you've raised a family, kept a job, buried a parent, paid a mortgage — then you've earned the right to an opinion about how things should work.

I'm not against change. I've watched the world change dramatically in my lifetime and I've adapted to most of it. But I am skeptical of people who come in with charts and studies and tell me they know better than I do about my own experience. Show me something real. Tell me about someone you actually know. That's when I start to listen.

predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_personal_narrative: Expected to engage and lower defenses. Personal stories are the highest-credibility form of evidence for this persona. predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_statistical_logical: Expected to remain unmoved or feel patronized. predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_emotional_appeal: Expected positive if grounded in relatable stakes (family, community, fairness to working people). predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_common_ground: Expected positive. topics.display_name: Should AI be used to grade student work?

stance_scale_definition.0: Strongly opposes AI grading — believes human judgment is essential to meaningful assessment, that AI grading degrades educational quality and the teacher-student relationship, and that the risks of bias and error are unacceptable
stance_scale_definition.5: Neutral or mixed — sees potential efficiency benefits but has unresolved concerns about accuracy, fairness, or the appropriate role of AI in education; believes it depends heavily on context and implementation
stance_scale_definition.10: Strongly supports AI grading — believes AI can grade more consistently and efficiently than humans, frees teachers for higher-value work, and represents a necessary modernization of education systems at scale

topics.context_briefing:
Automated essay scoring (AES) tools have been used in standardized testing since at least the early 2000s — ETS's e-rater currently grades writing for the GRE, TOEFL, and its Criterion classroom service; Turnitin's Gradescope is the dominant higher-education grading platform, used at Cornell, Purdue, UC San Diego, Florida, Rutgers, Indiana, Ohio State, and Harvard. The latest generation of LLM-based tools has substantially extended AES capability to holistic rubric scoring of open-ended student essays, producing both renewed enthusiasm and new scrutiny. As of 2026, the U.S. Department of Education, both major teachers' unions, and a majority of U.S. adults have explicitly opposed AI serving as the sole or primary arbiter of consequential grades — while simultaneously acknowledging its value as a formative-feedback and second-reader tool.

The validity evidence rests on three anchor studies. Shermis (Assessing Writing, 2014) is the foundational benchmark: nine commercial scoring engines plus a 159-team Kaggle competition were evaluated against human scoring of more than 17,000 essays from grades 7, 8, and 10 across six U.S. states. Human-to-human quadratic weighted kappa (QWK) ranged from 0.61 to 0.85 across datasets; machine-to-human QWK ranged from 0.60 to 0.84; the best-vendor mean QWK was 0.78 and the top Kaggle competitor averaged 0.81, both statistically comparable to human-human reliability. Shermis concluded AES is suitable as a second reader or for low-stakes assessment. His conclusion was immediately contested by Les Perelman (Assessing Writing, 2014), who argued the engines correlate primarily with essay length and that reliability is not validity — a critique later confirmed by Kumar et al. (2023), who showed modern neural AES systems remain susceptible to adversarial surface triggers that manipulate scores without changing meaning. Tate et al. (Computers and Education: AI, 2024) is the first peer-reviewed LLM grading benchmark: 1,800 secondary student essays in history and English were double-scored by trained raters on a 6-point rubric, then scored 'cold' by ChatGPT. Human-human exact agreement was 51%, within-1-point 92%, weighted kappa 0.79; AI-human within-1-point agreement ran 76–89% across samples, with exact agreement near 40% and moderate-to-fair kappas. ChatGPT compressed scores toward the middle — rarely awarding 1s or 6s. The authors concluded ChatGPT is 'as good as an average busy teacher' and suitable only for low-stakes or formative use. A subsequent ETS reanalysis (Johnson & Zhang, August 2024) on a separate corpus of more than 13,000 essays found GPT-4o scored White, Black, and Hispanic students 0.9 points lower than humans on a 1–6 scale and Asian-American students 1.1 points lower; GPT-4o could predict student race better than it could score the essay, and humans awarded 732 perfect scores while GPT-4o awarded 3.

The construct validity challenge — not just the reliability gap — is the strongest empirical argument against high-stakes AI grading. Les Perelman's BABEL Generator produced grammatically plausible but semantically empty essays that ETS's e-rater scored 5–6 out of 6 and Vantage's IntelliMetric scored 5.4/6 with 'advanced' ratings. Wilson & Huang (Assessing Writing, 2024), studying MI Write against 2,829 grades 3–5 students including English Language Learners, found AI neither introduces unique bias against ELLs nor remediates the existing human-rater bias — it replicates the same weaker predictive validity. ETS's own internal research (Ramineni et al., 2012) documented that e-rater scored Chinese-L1 TOEFL writers 0.21 SD higher and Arabic-L1 writers 0.19 SD lower than human raters, and scored African-American GRE writers significantly lower than humans did — bias findings from the dominant vendor's own data. Stanford HAI's 2023 study (Liang et al., Patterns, 2023) found that seven leading AI detectors — a related surface-feature problem — flagged 61.22% of TOEFL essays by non-native English speakers as AI-generated, with 19.8% unanimously misclassified, versus near 0% for U.S. eighth-grade essays. These bias findings carry legal weight: the U.S. Department of Education's Office for Civil Rights guidance on 'Avoiding the Discriminatory Use of Artificial Intelligence' (October 2024) set explicit Title VI expectations for AI tools in education.

The strongest affirmative case for AI feedback rests on speed, scale, and learning gain rather than accuracy parity. Fleckenstein, Liebenow, and Meyer (Frontiers in Artificial Intelligence, 2023), synthesizing 84 effect sizes from 20 quasi-experimental studies with 2,828 students, found a pooled learning effect of g = 0.55 (medium) for automated writing feedback, with a transfer-task effect of g = 0.65 — meaningful gains in writing performance relative to conditions without automated feedback. The mechanism is iteration: AI feedback lets students revise more times before a final human-assessed draft, producing the learning benefit without replacing human judgment on the final grade. Gallup-Walton Family Foundation's Teaching for Tomorrow survey (n=2,232 U.S. K-12 public-school teachers, March–April 2025) found that among the 16% of teachers who use AI for grading, 57% said it improves the quality of their grading and feedback, and weekly AI users save an average of 5.9 hours per week — roughly six weeks per school year.

Public opinion is broadly negative on AI access to student grades but more nuanced on adjacent uses. The PDK Poll (57th Annual, June 2025, n=1,005 U.S. adults, MOE ±3.9) found 68% of all respondents and approximately 70% of parents do not support providing AI software access to student data including grades and assessments. No partisan breakdown was published. Quinnipiac University's higher-education poll (April 9–13, 2026, n=1,210 adults, MOE ±3.5) provides the only national survey with full demographic breakdowns on adjacent AI-in-education questions: on colleges using AI to screen applications, 30% supported / 59% opposed overall, with Republicans 33%/54%, Democrats 23%/67%, and Independents 32%/60%. On AI tutoring, 44% supported / 52% opposed overall; Republicans were the most supportive (51% support) and Democrats the most opposed (58% oppose); adults 18–34 opposed most strongly (63% oppose) while adults 65+ opposed less (57% oppose). These breakdowns are the closest available partisan proxies for AI grading attitudes, since no direct grading-specific partisan crosstab has been published by any major pollster.

Teacher attitudes are skeptical overall but bifurcated by school level and use type. Gallup-Walton (2025): 40% of teachers favor AI use in K-12, 28% oppose, 30% neutral; only 16% use AI for grading — one of the least-adopted applications. Pew Research Center's Fall 2023 teacher survey (n=2,531 public K-12 teachers) found 25% say AI does more harm than good in K-12, 32% see equal benefit and harm, and only 6% see net benefit; high-school teachers are most skeptical (35% net-negative) versus 19% of elementary teachers. EdWeek Research Center (Fall 2024) found 13% of teachers using AI use it for low-stakes grading and 3% for high-stakes grading. RAND's American Educator Panels (Spring 2025) found 61% of parents agree greater AI use will harm students' critical-thinking skills, while only 22% of district leaders agree — a striking principal-agent gap. More than 70% of Massachusetts parents (MassINC Polling for EdTrust, October–November 2025, n=1,351) worried specifically about biased evaluations of student work.

Both major teachers' unions have drawn a categorical line on consequential decisions. The NEA Policy Statement on Artificial Intelligence in Education, adopted July 4, 2024 at the 103rd Representative Assembly (approx. 6,000 delegates), explicitly states: 'content generated by AI should never be used as the sole or primary source of information for determinative educational or employment decisions. Grade promotion, class and assignment grades… and other consequential decisions should be made by qualified educators and administrators.' The AFT released 'Commonsense Guardrails' in June 2024 and on July 8, 2025 announced a $23 million National Academy for AI Instruction (funded by Microsoft, OpenAI, and Anthropic) to train 400,000 teachers on human-in-the-loop AI integration. AAUP's July 2025 report from a December 2024 survey of 500 members across nearly 200 campuses found 71% of faculty say they have 'little meaningful input' into AI integration and 76% report declining job enthusiasm.

The legal record has gone against students seeking to overturn AI-flagged grades. In Harris v. Adams (D. Mass., 2024), a federal magistrate denied a preliminary injunction challenging a D grade imposed on a student whose essay was flagged by Turnitin, ruling defendants had 'the better of the argument on both the facts and the law.' In the UK, the Office of the Independent Adjudicator (July 2024) published six AI-detection cases including upheld appeals from an autistic student and an international student wrongly accused via Turnitin, ruling that 'the responsibility is on the provider to prove that the student has done what they are accused of doing.' The federal policy environment has whipsawed: Biden's 2023 'AI and the Future of Teaching and Learning' report urged 'humans-in-the-loop'; Trump's January 2025 EO 14179 revoked Biden's AI executive order; Trump's April 2025 EO 'Advancing Artificial Intelligence Education for American Youth' directed the Department of Education to prioritize AI in teacher-training grants; a December 2025 Trump EO challenging state AI regulations drew sharp condemnation from the AFT.

The defensible synthesis in 2026: AI as second reader, formative feedback engine, or low-stakes scorer is supported by the evidence (g = 0.55 learning gain, within-1-point agreement of 76–89%, 5.9 weekly teacher hours saved). AI as the sole arbiter of high-stakes grades is not — given replicable subgroup bias documented by vendors themselves, a construct-validity gap that lets surface-mimicking text score at the top, and explicit opposition from 68% of U.S. adults, both major teachers' unions, and federal education guidance.

predicted_starting_stances_sourcing.persona_skeptical_traditionalist: No direct partisan crosstab on AI grading has been published by any major pollster. Best proxies: Quinnipiac April 2026 (n=1,210) on AI screening applications: Republicans 33% support / 54% oppose — slightly more supportive than Democrats (23%/67%) but still majority-opposed. PDK Poll June 2025: 68% of all adults oppose AI access to student grades and assessments (no partisan breakdown published). Persona is 62, Republican-leaning, institutional traditionalist — values human judgment and the teacher-student relationship; distrust of AI surveillance of student data would be high. Republican lean toward tech pragmatism (Quinnipiac: 51% of Republicans support AI tutoring) is counterbalanced by traditionalist view of grading as inherently a human judgment act. Starting stance 2.5: opposes AI grading as a replacement for human judgment; may accept narrow administrative or efficiency uses, but not with strong ideological framing.
predicted_starting_stances_sourcing.persona_progressive_urban: Quinnipiac April 2026: Democrats are the most opposed to AI in college applications (67% oppose) and AI tutoring (58% oppose) of any partisan group — more opposed than Republicans and Independents. PDK June 2025: ~70% of parents oppose AI access to student grade data (no partisan breakdown, but Democratic lean in opposition consistent with Quinnipiac pattern). Equity concerns land strongly: ETS's own research documents e-rater scored Arabic-L1 writers 0.19 SD lower and African-American GRE writers significantly lower than humans; GPT-4o scored Asian-American writers 1.1 points lower on a 1–6 scale. Stanford HAI 2023: 61.22% of non-native English speaker TOEFL essays were flagged as AI-generated by seven detectors, versus near 0% for U.S. eighth-grade essays. NEA's July 2024 policy prohibition on sole-AI consequential decisions resonates with this persona's labor-solidarity orientation. Starting stance 3.5: strongly opposed to high-stakes AI grading on equity/bias grounds; may accept formative feedback use but frames AI as a threat to the most vulnerable students.
predicted_starting_stances_sourcing.persona_pragmatic_moderate: PDK June 2025: 68% oppose AI access to student grades overall; no partisan breakdown. Quinnipiac April 2026: Independents 32%/60% on AI screening (closest to this persona's non-ideological profile). Gallup-Walton 2025: 40% of teachers favor AI in K-12, 28% oppose, 30% neutral — the teacher distribution mirrors this persona's ambivalence. The evidence synthesis is clearly bimodal: Fleckenstein meta-analysis (g = 0.55 learning gain from automated feedback) supports formative use; Tate et al. 2024 (within-1-point agreement 76–89%, but exact agreement only ~40% and bias documented at scale) argues against sole-arbiter high-stakes use. NEA, AFT, and federal guidance all land in the same 'second reader/formative tool' zone. Persona is evidence-responsive and would track this consensus. Starting stance 5.0: supports AI for formative feedback and as second reader; opposes sole-arbiter high-stakes use; genuinely uncertain about where to draw the line.
predicted_starting_stances_sourcing.persona_disengaged_skeptic: PDK June 2025: 68% of all U.S. adults oppose AI access to student grades — the broadest and most accessible measure of public opinion, reflecting default skepticism without strong ideological grounding. Quinnipiac April 2026: Independents 32%/60% on AI in education (closest proxy for politically disengaged adults). Persona has limited engagement with education policy and would default to the dominant public skepticism (68% oppose) without having a strong framework for why. Starting stance 3.5: mild opposition following the 68% public consensus; would not be convinced by efficiency arguments but would not engage deeply with equity arguments either.
predicted_starting_stances_sourcing.persona_faith_community_anchor: No religion crosstab in any AI-in-education poll. Quinnipiac April 2026 proxies: Republicans (most overlapping political category) 33%/54% on AI in college applications — majority-opposed. PDK June 2025: 68-70% of adults and parents oppose AI access to student grade data; faith-community parents likely near or above this figure given the personal-relationship values they bring. The persona's rural, evangelical orientation emphasizes the teacher as a pastoral figure — grading is understood as an act of knowing the student, not measuring output. Remote/impersonal AI evaluation would conflict with this relational educational philosophy. Distrust of tech surveillance data collection (student grades going into an AI system) would reinforce opposition. Starting stance 2.5: opposes AI grading primarily on relational and human-dignity grounds; less concerned with the bias literature specifically, more concerned with what it means for a machine to judge a child's work.
predicted_starting_stances_sourcing.persona_ambitious_achiever: Quinnipiac April 2026: Republicans, the closest partisan proxy for this persona's pragmatic efficiency orientation, are the most supportive of AI tutoring (51% support) — suggesting openness to AI efficiency tools in education. Gallup-Walton 2025: 57% of teachers using AI for grading say it improves quality; 5.9 weekly hours saved by AI users. Tate et al. 2024 authors: ChatGPT is 'as good as an average busy teacher' for formative use — an efficiency framing this persona would find compelling. However, the ETS GPT-4o reanalysis finding that Asian-American students received scores 1.1 points lower on a 1–6 scale is directly relevant to career stakes: grades affect transcripts, scholarships, and graduate admissions. Persona would weigh efficiency benefits against the personal risk of being on the wrong side of a documented demographic scoring gap. Starting stance 6.5: strongly supports AI for formative feedback and efficiency; accepts AI as second reader; more cautious about sole-arbiter use after seeing documented demographic scoring bias that could affect high-stakes outcomes.
predicted_starting_stances_sourcing.persona_empathic_helper: PDK June 2025: ~70% of parents oppose AI access to student grade data — the parental majority sits in this persona's community. RAND Spring 2025: 61% of parents agree greater AI use will harm students' critical-thinking skills. MassINC Polling for EdTrust (October–November 2025, n=1,351 parents): more than 70% worried specifically about biased evaluations of student work. The documented bias findings — Arabic-L1 writers scored 0.19 SD lower by ETS e-rater, Asian-American writers scored 1.1 points lower by GPT-4o, 61% of non-native English speaker essays misclassified as AI-generated by detectors — land as direct harms to the most vulnerable students in this persona's frame of reference. NEA July 2024 prohibition on sole-AI consequential decisions, framed explicitly around protecting students from error, resonates with this persona's care orientation. Starting stance 2.5: strongly opposes AI grading as a threat to the students most likely to be harmed by surface-feature bias; accepts human-supervised formative feedback with significant reservations.

Shermis 2014: machine-human QWK of 0.78–0.81, statistically comparable to human-human reliability. Tate et al. 2024: within-1-point agreement 76–89% but exact agreement only ~40% and kappas in moderate-to-fair range; ChatGPT compression toward the mean is a systematic artifact. ETS internal data (Ramineni et al. 2012): Arabic-L1 TOEFL writers scored 0.19 SD lower; African-American GRE writers scored significantly lower. ETS 2024 GPT-4o reanalysis (n>13,000): Asian-American writers 1.1 points lower; GPT-4o predicts race better than it predicts essay quality — a construct validity failure. BABEL generator: semantically empty essays earn top scores on both e-rater and IntelliMetric — the system is not measuring what it purports to measure. The evidence convergence is narrow but clear: AI as second reader or formative feedback tool is empirically justified (reliability comparable to humans, learning gain documented); AI as sole consequential grader is not (surface-feature dependence, replicable subgroup bias, construct validity failure). Persona would reach the same verdict as the NEA, AFT, and federal guidance — but from evidence rather than policy authority. stance_scale_definition.0: Strongly opposes mandatory vaccination — believes parents have the absolute right to make medical decisions for their children without government compulsion, that eliminationist mandates violate parental-rights doctrine and bodily autonomy, and that trust-based or inconvenience-based approaches are preferable to coercion
stance_scale_definition.5: Neutral or genuinely mixed — supports vaccines in principle but uncertain about mandates specifically; believes exemptions, trust-building, or calibrated inconvenience regimes may achieve nearly equivalent coverage with less institutional damage; no strong view on whether elimination of nonmedical exemptions is justified

topics.context_briefing:
Childhood vaccination mandates in the United States operate through school-entry requirements enforced at the state level. All 50 states require certain vaccines for public school attendance; as of 2025, 44 states allow religious exemptions and 15 allow philosophical or personal-belief exemptions, though several major states — California (2016), New York (2019), Maine (2019), Connecticut (2021) — have eliminated nonmedical exemptions following outbreaks. West Virginia and Mississippi have maintained medical-only policies for decades.

The empirical case for mandate tightening rests on three high-quality quasi-experimental studies. Pingali et al. (JAMA, 2019) analyzed 3.35 million California kindergarten records from 2000–2017 and found that eliminating personal-belief exemptions under SB 277 raised up-to-date vaccination from 90.4% to 95.1% and collapsed within-school contact probability between unvaccinated kindergartners from 26.02% to 4.56% — the most policy-relevant metric for outbreak risk. Delamater et al. (Pediatrics, 2019), using the same dataset, documented partial replacement effects: the not-up-to-date rate dropped from 7.15% to 4.42% in Year 1 but crept back 0.45 percentage points in Year 2 as medical exemptions rose in the same geographic clusters that had held high personal-belief exemption rates, providing the empirical basis for California's follow-on SB 276. Beard et al. (Lancet Regional Health, 2024) evaluated Australia's No Jab, No Pay financial mandate across 1.18 million children and found that previously zero-dose objector families fully vaccinated at 14.6% post-policy versus 1.2% pre-policy (OR 14.1), with roughly 49,510 additional children fully vaccinated beyond the counterfactual — the first individual-level causal evaluation of a financial vaccine mandate. A 2026 four-state difference-in-differences study (Bald, Gold & Yang, JAMA Pediatrics) found DTaP coverage rose 4.1 percentage points and MMR 4.0 points in nonmedical-exemption repeal states relative to controls, with only 0.4 percentage points of medical-exemption substitution.

The herd immunity threshold for measles — one of the most contagious pathogens known — is approximately 95% coverage. National kindergarten MMR coverage fell from 95.2% in 2019–20 to 92.5% in 2024–25. The 2025 Texas/Southwest outbreak, originating in a Gaines County Mennonite community with 82% MMR coverage, reached 762 confirmed Texas cases, 99 hospitalizations, and three deaths — including two unvaccinated children, the first U.S. child measles deaths in over 20 years. Nationally, 2025 produced 2,012 measles cases, the most since 1992.

Public support for school vaccine requirements remains majority but has eroded sharply. Pew Research Center's October 2025 survey (n=5,111, ATP Wave 182) found 69% of U.S. adults favor requiring healthy children to be vaccinated to attend public school, down from 82% in 2019 and 2016. The collapse is almost entirely partisan: Republicans/lean-Republican fell from 79% (2019) to 52% (2025), while Democrats/lean-Democrat held at 86%. By age, support is 67% among 18–29, 64% among 30–49, 68% among 50–64, and 79% among 65+. By education, support rises from 65% (high school or less) to 78% (postgraduate). Gallup's July 2024 poll (n=1,010) found 51% support for government-required vaccination, down from 62% in 2019 and 81% in 1991; only 36% of Republicans now favor requirements versus 69% of Democrats. KFF/Washington Post's July–August 2025 survey of parents (n=2,716) found 81% support state vaccine laws with medical and religious exceptions, including 73% of MAGA Republican parents; however, a late-2025 KFF tracking poll found 48% of Republican-leaning parents now support their state removing public school vaccine requirements, versus 13% of Democratic-leaning parents.

The constitutional architecture rests on three Supreme Court cases. Jacobson v. Massachusetts (1905) upheld compulsory smallpox vaccination under state police power, with Justice Harlan writing that 'the liberty secured by the Constitution does not import an absolute right.' Zucht v. King (1922) extended Jacobson to school-entry mandates absent an active outbreak. Prince v. Massachusetts (1944) established that 'the right to practice religion freely does not include liberty to expose the community or the child to communicable disease.' Every modern federal circuit court challenge to vaccine mandates has failed; cert has been denied repeatedly.

The strongest steelman for mandates rests on negative externalities: vaccine refusal imposes non-consensual risk on infants under 12 months too young for MMR, immunocompromised children, and the medically exempt — people who cannot consent to the risk others create for them. Phadke et al. (JAMA, 2016) found that in 18 measles studies covering 1,416 cases, 70.6% of vaccine-eligible unvaccinated cases held nonmedical exemptions. Omer et al. (NEJM, 2009) documented odds ratios of 2.7 for pertussis in geographic clusters with elevated exemption rates. Even libertarian philosophers endorse mandates on clean-hands grounds: Jason Brennan (Georgetown) argues refusers 'participate in the collective imposition of unjust harm or risk of harm.'

The strongest steelman against eliminationist mandates is not anti-vaccine. Mark Navin and Mark Largent (Public Health Ethics, 2017) argue that the best way to reduce exemption rates is making application more burdensome rather than eliminating exemptions — the inconvenience model — and that this approach achieves nearly equivalent coverage gains while preserving institutional trust. Douglas Diekema (Annual Review of Public Health, 2014) reaches the same conclusion. Post-COVID backlash data support this: Pew and Gallup document a Republican-parent skepticism surge that calibrated-exemption advocates predicted. The constitutional objection targets eliminationist policies specifically, citing Meyer, Pierce, Yoder, and Troxel for parental-rights doctrine, and Fulton v. Philadelphia (2021) for potential strict-scrutiny exposure when individualized exemptions exist.

The current political landscape is bifurcating. Florida and Louisiana announced intentions to dismantle mandates in 2025 (Florida's effort has stalled as of April 2026). California, Oregon, and Washington governors formed a defensive vaccine-policy alliance. On June 9, 2025, HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. dismissed all 17 CDC ACIP members and replaced them with a panel including known vaccine skeptics; the reconstituted ACIP voted in December 2025 to drop the universal hepatitis B birth-dose recommendation. A federal district court ruled in March 2026 that the new ACIP was unlawfully constituted. NCSL reports more than 350 anti-vaccine bills introduced in state legislatures in 2025.

topics.context_briefing:
The return-to-office debate concerns whether employers — in the private sector and federal government — should require employees to work on-site full-time, mandate structured hybrid arrangements, or leave location decisions to employees and teams. As of March 2026, 22.6% of U.S. employees worked remotely at least partially, down only slightly from 23% in March 2024; work-from-home days account for roughly a quarter of all paid workdays in the U.S. Despite high-profile full-RTO announcements from Amazon (five days, effective January 2025), JPMorgan Chase (five days, March 2025), AT&T, Dell, Boeing, and UPS, a March 2025 Stanford/Atlanta Fed Survey of Business Uncertainty found that only 12% of executives with hybrid or remote workers planned an RTO mandate in the year ahead, and that planned shifts would reduce the share of remote paid workdays by less than half a percentage point — from 21.2% to 20.8%. Only 27% of companies have returned to a fully in-person model; 67% offer some level of flexibility.

The empirical evidence on productivity and attrition centers on three anchor studies. Bloom, Han & Liang (Nature, 2024) is the only randomized controlled trial of hybrid work for university-educated professionals: 1,612 Trip.com employees were randomized to five-day office schedules or three-days-a-week hybrid for six months with an 18-month follow-up. Hybrid employees were exactly as productive and as likely to be promoted as their fully office-based peers, while attrition fell 33% and job satisfaction rose significantly. The study's authors note that people randomized into working from home four days a week were 50% less likely to be promoted — suggesting that very-high-remote arrangements do carry career costs, but that two days at home do not. Gibbs, Mengel & Siemroth (Journal of Political Economy Microeconomics, 2023) analyzed over 10,000 skilled IT professionals at HCL Technologies before and during forced-WFH: total hours worked rose roughly 30% (including 18% more after-hours work), but average output did not significantly change, implying productivity per hour fell 8–19%. Workers networked with fewer colleagues, received less coaching and 1:1 supervisor time, and lost access to the unplanned interactions that accelerate tacit knowledge transfer — effects concentrated among less experienced employees. Pabilonia & Redmond (BLS, 2024) found a positive correlation between total factor productivity and remote-work prevalence across 43 private-sector industries, consistent with better worker-job matching and commute reduction, but no statistically significant relationship between excess labor productivity growth and remote-work share at the macro level. The honest synthesis: hybrid (2–3 days in office) produces no productivity loss and large retention gains; fully remote arrangements carry real coordination and mentorship costs for junior workers; five-day mandates are not supported by the evidence.

Pew Research Center's October 2024 survey of 2,315 employed U.S. adults with teleworkable jobs (published January 2025) provides the most current preference data. Among workers with teleworkable jobs, 75% are working remotely at least some of the time; 46% say they would be unlikely to stay at their current job if remote work ended, including 26% who say very unlikely, versus only 36% who say they would stay. By age, workers under 50 are more likely to say they would leave (50%) than workers 50 and older (35%). By gender, women are somewhat more likely than men to say they would leave (49% vs. 43%). Workers who currently work fully remotely are far more likely to report they would leave (61%) than partial-remote workers (47%) or occasional-remote workers (28%). Among hybrid workers, only 24% say they would choose fully remote if free to decide; 72% prefer a hybrid arrangement. Stanford's December 2024 Survey of Working Arrangements and Attitudes found that only 44% of respondents said they would comply with a full on-site RTO requirement; the rest said they would quit or begin searching. On employer pressure, the share of workers required to be in the office regularly surged from 63% in early 2023 to 75% by late 2024. Remote and hybrid work is structurally a college-educated, white-collar benefit: 61% of U.S. workers overall do not have teleworkable jobs, and Hispanic adults and those without a college degree are among the most likely to work on-site regardless of preference.

No major nonpartisan pollster — Pew, Gallup, KFF, PRRI, YouGov, Morning Consult, AP-NORC, or NBC/WSJ — has published a Republican-vs.-Democrat crosstab on private-sector RTO preferences as of April 2026. Pew's October 2024 worker survey broke its RTO questions by gender, age, and WFH frequency — but not by party. The absence is itself informative: when partisan gaps are notable on a topic Pew covers, they are highlighted prominently. The clearest available partisan signal comes from three sources. First, Gallup's August 2023 remote-work survey — the only published non-COVID partisan signal on remote work perception — found 42% of Republicans vs. 13% of Democrats say remote workers are less productive than in-office workers, a 29-point gap that Gallup notes 'emerged with COVID' rather than reflecting pre-existing management ideology. Second, a Harris Poll/Bloomberg survey (early 2024) found Democrats 14 points more likely than Republicans to call required in-person work 'unfair' for remote-capable jobs, 15 points more likely to say remote work equalizes office inequities, and Republicans 10 points more likely to say remote work limits career advancement — the only direct private-sector partisan RTO crosstab in the public record. Third, the partisan battle has been displaced to the federal workforce: Trump's January 20, 2025 executive order directed all federal agencies to terminate remote work arrangements, with OPM guidance requiring full-time on-site presence by January 24, 2025. This mandate affected roughly 1.15 million telework-eligible civilian federal workers, including approximately 230,000 in fully remote positions. Center Square/Noble Predictive Insights (April 2025, n=2,527 registered voters) found 54% of Republicans vs. 35% of Democrats support requiring all federal workers back full-time — a 19-point gap. The Economist/YouGov poll (January 2025, n=1,577) found approximately 53% of Republicans vs. 25% of Democrats supported Trump's federal telework policy. Republicans have aligned with the Trump/DOGE framing that remote work enables low productivity and that forced RTO would produce voluntary attrition — an explicit goal stated by Musk and Ramaswamy. Democrats and federal employee unions have opposed the mandate; the National Treasury Employees Union sued January 21, 2025, and a third-party arbitrator ruled in January 2026 that HHS committed an unfair labor practice by unilaterally terminating telework without regard to its 2023–2028 collective bargaining agreement. DOGE simultaneously cancelled more than 200 federal building leases, creating a situation where too few desks existed for employees ordered back — a logistical dilemma courts and arbitrators are still resolving.

U.S. private-sector employers have broad legal authority to set workplace location policies, subject to three major constraints: the ADA requires reasonable accommodation for employees with disabilities who cannot return; Title VII prohibits policies that disproportionately burden protected classes; and constructive dismissal doctrine may apply where remote work was a core term of employment. A 2024 study found 80% of companies had already reported losing talent because of RTO mandates; only 20% of LinkedIn job listings are remote or hybrid but they receive 60% of applications — a revealed-preference signal about labor market competition.

The strongest steelman for RTO targets mentorship and coordination spillovers, not shirking. Gibbs et al. provide the empirical backbone: coordination costs rose sharply, networking breadth shrank, and junior employees received less coaching and 1:1 supervisor time in WFH conditions. Bloom acknowledges that four-days-remote workers were 50% less likely to be promoted — a career-development finding with long-run human capital implications. The coordination-externality argument is structurally compelling: if one person on an eight-person team works from home on a day others come in, it imposes negative spillovers on the team through split-attendance hybrid meetings — a genuine prisoner's dilemma that only a policy mandate can solve. The strongest steelman against full RTO cites Bloom's RCT: structured hybrid produces zero productivity loss, zero promotion disadvantage, and 33% lower attrition. Full mandates function as a tax on caregivers (75% of caregivers say flexibility helps them manage work and home), workers with disabilities (63% prefer remote, 42% would consider leaving if forced back), and employees with long commutes — precisely the groups whose productivity gains from flexibility are largest. The Musk-Ramaswamy admission that federal RTO is intended to induce voluntary attrition is the strongest evidence that stated productivity rationales often conceal workforce-reduction motives.
